content.text: Every coding agent ultimately needs to act on files — reading existing code for context, writing new modules, and making surgical edits to fix bugs or implement features.

are the structured interfaces that bridge the gap between an agent's reasoning and the actual bytes on disk. They give agents a safe, auditable, and token-efficient way to interact with a project's filesystem without requiring shell access or the risk of ad-hoc script execution.

A file editing tool is a function exposed to an AI agent through its tool-use interface. Each tool has a defined schema (name, parameters, return shape) and performs exactly one filesystem operation: reading a file, overwriting a file, replacing a substring within a file, or searching for patterns across many files. The agent calls these tools as structured JSON actions, and the tool executor — the host process or sandbox — carries out the actual I/O.
The canonical set of file editing tools found in production coding agents consists of four primitives:
(fetch file contents, optionally windowed by line range),
(create or overwrite a file with new content),
(replace an exact string with a replacement string inside a file), and
(scan files matching a glob pattern for a regex). Some systems add derivatives such as
, but the core four handle the vast majority of code-editing workflows.

These tools appear as tool definitions in the model's context window — usually a JSON schema listing the function name, a description, and each parameter's type and purpose. The model emits a tool-call block when it decides a filesystem operation is needed, and the host deserializes and executes it, returning the result back into the conversation.

Before file editing tools existed, AI coding assistants could only suggest changes — they produced diff-like text that a developer had to copy, review, and apply manually. This made the workflow slow and error-prone, and it meant that multi-step tasks such as "refactor this module, update the tests, fix the import in five other files" required constant human intervention between each step.
File editing tools collapse that loop. An agent can read a file to understand its structure, identify the exact lines that need changing, apply a
call to patch those lines, then immediately read the result back to verify correctness — all autonomously. The human reviews the completed diff rather than shepherding each micro-step.
The design of the tools matters as much as their existence. A
that overwrites the entire file is simple to implement but forces the agent to hold the entire file content in context, which is expensive and fragile for large codebases.
addresses this by allowing the agent to send only the changed region, dramatically reducing token consumption and the probability that the model hallucinates unrelated code during a large write.
Reading files with line-range windows (e.g., lines 100-150) keeps context small. Surgical replacements via
avoid regenerating hundreds of unchanged lines.
Every file edit is a discrete, logged tool call with explicit parameters. Replaying the call log gives a step-by-step record of every change the agent made.
is a pure read — it cannot corrupt state. Agents can safely call it multiple times to refresh context after a write.
The four primitives compose into higher-order workflows: search, then read, then replace, then read-back-to-verify is a standard edit cycle that any agent loop can execute.

The agent reasons in natural language about what change is needed; the tool execution happens in a deterministic host process. Bugs in the model's reasoning do not silently corrupt files — each call must pass schema validation before execution.

call fails because the old string was not found, the error message returns to the agent, which can re-read the file and retry with the correct string. The feedback loop keeps the agent on track without human intervention.

If the target string contains trailing whitespace, a different indentation level, or was changed by an earlier tool call, the replacement will fail. This is a common source of retry loops in multi-step edits.
File editing tools operate one file at a time. An agent renaming a symbol across twenty files is making twenty separate tool calls; a crash partway through leaves the codebase partially refactored.
The tools work directly on the filesystem. If the agent makes a bad edit, rolling back requires git or a prior snapshot. The tools themselves do not maintain undo history.

Reading a 50,000-line file in one call is impractical — the context window cannot hold it and the model will not read every line carefully. Agents need windowed reads and search tools to navigate large files, adding complexity to the planning logic.
A coding agent with unrestricted write access can delete or overwrite arbitrary files. The tool executor must enforce sandboxing to limit the blast radius of a misbehaving agent.
File editing tools operate on UTF-8 text. Binary assets (images, compiled artifacts, database files) cannot be meaningfully read or edited through these interfaces.

At runtime, file editing tools follow a strict request-response cycle. The model emits a tool call as a JSON block embedded in its output. The host extracts the call, validates its parameters against the tool's JSON schema, executes the underlying filesystem operation, and injects the result back into the conversation as a tool result message. The model then continues generation with that result in context.
The read tool opens the file at the specified path and returns its content as a UTF-8 string. Most implementations accept optional

(maximum lines to return) parameters. When both are omitted the whole file is returned; when the file exceeds a practical token budget (commonly 2,000-4,000 lines), the host applies a hard cap and annotates the response with a truncation notice.

string and atomically overwrites the file. Most implementations write to a temp file in the same directory and rename it to avoid partial writes. If the directory does not exist, implementations typically create it recursively. The return value is a success/failure status and optionally the number of bytes written.
The replace tool is the workhorse of incremental editing. It accepts
(replacement text). The tool reads the current file content, finds the first occurrence of
using exact string matching, and replaces it with
appears multiple times, most implementations reject the call with an error to prevent ambiguous edits; the agent must provide more context (surrounding lines) to make the match unique.

The tool returns the number of lines affected, or a unified diff snippet, so the model can verify the change without issuing a second read call. Whitespace is significant: the match is byte-for-byte, not semantically normalized. Leading indentation, trailing newlines, and Windows vs. Unix line endings all matter.
The search tool accepts a regex pattern and an optional file glob (e.g.,

). It uses ripgrep-style matching under the hood and returns each hit as a record of (file path, line number, context lines). The result set is typically capped to avoid flooding the context window. The agent uses search to locate symbols, understand call sites, or identify which files need edits before opening them.

The diagram below shows how file editing tools fit into the broader coding-agent architecture. The AI model sits at the top and emits tool calls; a dispatcher validates and routes each call to the appropriate file I/O handler; each handler operates on the local filesystem and returns its result up the stack.

attrs.alt: File editing tools architecture: AI agent → tool dispatcher → read/write/str_replace/search → local filesystem

content.text: The key insight in this architecture is that the model never touches the filesystem directly. All I/O passes through the dispatcher, which can enforce access controls (path allowlists, read-only mode, sandbox chroot) without the model being aware of them. This separation is what makes it practical to give a model broad file-editing permissions while still limiting the blast radius of an errant call.
File editing tools are the right choice whenever the task requires persistent code changes, not just analysis or suggestion. The following scenarios are prime candidates:
The agent finds the error source via search, reads the relevant function, applies a targeted
Multi-file changes — adding a new API endpoint, wiring up a handler, and updating the router — all within a single agent loop.
Renaming a function across a codebase, updating deprecated API calls, or migrating to a new library version.
Generating boilerplate files (config, tests, stubs) from a template and writing them with
An agent reads changed files (from git diff), reasons about correctness, then applies inline fixup edits.

Avoid file editing tools when the goal is purely analytical — code review without edits, explaining what code does — in those cases reading tools suffice and write tools add unnecessary risk. Also avoid them for binary assets, generated files that will be overwritten by the next build, or files under active editing by a human in the same session.

Implementing a minimal set of file editing tools for a coding agent involves three layers: the tool schema (what the model sees), the tool executor (the Python or TypeScript function that does the I/O), and the integration glue (how results flow back into the conversation). The following walkthrough covers all three.
Each tool is described as a JSON schema passed to the model. The schema must be precise enough for the model to call the tool correctly without needing natural-language inference for parameter names.

Executors are plain functions that receive validated parameters and return a string result. Returning a string (rather than a structured object) keeps the model's context clean and avoids schema mismatches.

import os, pathlib

WORKSPACE_ROOT = pathlib.Path("/workspace").resolve()

def _safe_path(path: str) -> pathlib.Path:
    # Resolve path and verify it stays inside WORKSPACE_ROOT
    resolved = (WORKSPACE_ROOT / path).resolve()
    if not str(resolved).startswith(str(WORKSPACE_ROOT)):
        raise PermissionError(f"Path escapes workspace: {path}")
    return resolved

def read_file(path: str, offset: int = None, limit: int = None) -> str:
    p = _safe_path(path)
    if not p.exists():
        return f"Error: file not found: {path}"
    lines = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace").splitlines(keepends=True)
    total = len(lines)
    start = (offset - 1) if offset else 0
    end = (start + limit) if limit else total
    window = lines[start:end]
    header = f"<file path='{path}' lines={start+1}-{min(end, total)} total={total}>\n"
    return header + "".join(window)

def write_file(path: str, content: str) -> str:
    p = _safe_path(path)
    p.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    tmp = p.with_suffix(p.suffix + ".tmp")
    tmp.write_text(content, encoding="utf-8")
    tmp.replace(p)   # atomic rename on POSIX
    lines = content.count("\n") + (1 if not content.endswith("\n") else 0)
    return f"Written {len(content)} bytes ({lines} lines) to {path}"

def str_replace_editor(path: str, old_str: str, new_str: str) -> str:
    p = _safe_path(path)
    original = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    count = original.count(old_str)
    if count == 0:
        return f"Error: old_str not found in {path}"
    if count > 1:
        return (
            f"Error: old_str appears {count} times in {path}. "
            "Provide more context lines to make it unique."
        )
    updated = original.replace(old_str, new_str, 1)
    tmp = p.with_suffix(p.suffix + ".tmp")
    tmp.write_text(updated, encoding="utf-8")
    tmp.replace(p)
    old_lines = old_str.count("\n") + 1
    new_lines = new_str.count("\n") + 1
    return f"Replaced {old_lines} line(s) with {new_lines} line(s) in {path}"

def search_files(pattern: str, glob: str = "**/*") -> str:
    import re
    results = []
    regex = re.compile(pattern, re.MULTILINE)
    for fp in sorted(WORKSPACE_ROOT.rglob(glob.lstrip("*/"))):
        if not fp.is_file(): continue
        try: text = fp.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore")
        except OSError: continue
        for m in regex.finditer(text):
            lineno = text[:m.start()].count("\n") + 1
            line = text.splitlines()[lineno - 1]
            results.append(f"{fp.relative_to(WORKSPACE_ROOT)}:{lineno}: {line.strip()}")
            if len(results) >= 50: return "\n".join(results) + "\n...(truncated)"
    return "\n".join(results) if results else "No matches found"
A simple single-file edit: the agent reads a Python function, discovers an off-by-one error in a slice, and patches it with

# Task: "Fix the bug in src/utils.py where get_page() returns one extra item"

# 1. Read the relevant section of the file
result = read_file("src/utils.py", offset=42, limit=15)
# Output:
# <file path='src/utils.py' lines=42-56 total=210>
# def get_page(items, page, size):
#     start = page * size
#     end = start + size + 1   # bug: +1 is off-by-one
#     return items[start:end]

# 2. Apply the fix — include the entire function for uniqueness
result = str_replace_editor(
    path="src/utils.py",
    old_str="""def get_page(items, page, size):
    start = page * size
    end = start + size + 1   # bug: +1 is off-by-one
    return items[start:end]""",
    new_str="""def get_page(items, page, size):
    start = page * size
    end = start + size
    return items[start:end]""",
)
# Output: "Replaced 4 line(s) with 4 line(s) in src/utils.py"

# 3. Read back to verify
result = read_file("src/utils.py", offset=42, limit=8)
# Confirms the corrected function is in place

A more realistic workflow: the agent adds a new REST endpoint to a FastAPI application, touching three files. It uses search to understand existing routing patterns, then makes targeted edits to each file.

# Task: "Add a /health endpoint that returns {status: ok, version: <app_version>}"

# Step 1: Find where routes are defined
hits = search_files(pattern=r"@(app|router)\.(get|post)", glob="**/*.py")
# Returns: src/api/routes.py:14: @router.get("/users")

# Step 2: Read the routes file to understand the pattern
routes_content = read_file("src/api/routes.py")

# Step 3: Read config to find where APP_VERSION is defined
config_content = read_file("src/config.py")
# Discovers: APP_VERSION = os.getenv("APP_VERSION", "0.1.0")

# Step 4: Add the import and the new endpoint
str_replace_editor(
    path="src/api/routes.py",
    old_str="from fastapi import APIRouter\n",
    new_str="from fastapi import APIRouter\nfrom src.config import APP_VERSION\n",
)
str_replace_editor(
    path="src/api/routes.py",
    old_str="# -- end of routes --",
    new_str=(
        '@router.get("/health", tags=["ops"])\n'
        'def health_check():\n'
        '    return {"status": "ok", "version": APP_VERSION}\n\n'
        '# -- end of routes --'
    ),
)

# Step 5: Write the test file
write_file("tests/test_health.py", """from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from src.main import app

client = TestClient(app)

def test_health_returns_ok():
    response = client.get("/health")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json()["status"] == "ok"
""")
Claude Code, Anthropic's terminal-based coding agent, uses exactly this tool set in production. Its
with one addition: it records each edit as a before/after pair that the user can review in a compact diff display. The
tool creates or overwrites a file after asking permission on the first write to each path. The
parameters and annotates the output with line numbers and a truncation notice when the file exceeds the display limit.

In a real session, an agent working through a task such as "implement the new billing webhook handler" issues on the order of 20-40 tool calls across 8-12 files. A typical breakdown: 12 reads (initial scan plus verification reads), 5 str_replace edits (targeted patches to existing handlers), 3 writes (new test file, new handler module, updated config), and 4 searches (finding call sites and import patterns). The entire session completes in under two minutes and produces a git diff that a human reviews in one pass.

The Aider coding agent (open source, widely used) takes a slightly different approach: it generates a full diff in the "SEARCH/REPLACE" format and applies all hunks in one step. This reduces round trips but requires the model to produce the entire replacement content, which is more susceptible to hallucination for large files. The tradeoff illustrates a core design choice: more granular tools (as used by Claude Code) give more error-recovery opportunities; fewer, larger operations (Aider's approach) reduce latency when the model is reliable.

At scale — for automated PR-review bots running against thousands of pull requests per day — the same tool primitives are used but the executor runs inside a containerized environment: each invocation spins up a fresh Docker container with the repository checked out, runs the agent, and discards the container. The filesystem isolation replaces the path-sanitization layer needed in single-process agents.
Using write_file when str_replace_editor would do
A common mistake is having the agent read an entire file and then call

with a slightly modified version. This wastes tokens, is fragile (the regenerated content may differ subtly from the original), and can silently alter formatting or whitespace that the model decided to 'improve' without being asked.

# BAD: regenerate the whole file to change one line
content = read_file("src/config.py")
# The model generates a new version of the entire 300-line file...
write_file("src/config.py", new_content)
# Risk: the model quietly reformatted 40 lines it didn't need to touch.

# GOOD: surgical patch
str_replace_editor(
    path="src/config.py",
    old_str="DEBUG = True",
    new_str="DEBUG = False",
)

Writing a new file without reading the existing one is only safe when the agent is creating a file from scratch. If the file might already exist, writing without reading can silently overwrite content. The agent should always issue a
first to confirm the file's current state before deciding whether to write or replace.
Agents sometimes identify a bug in a compiled output file (e.g., a bundled
or a generated Protobuf stub) and apply a fix there, not realizing the file will be regenerated on the next build. Always check whether the target file has a generation header comment or is listed in
before editing it — the real fix belongs in the source file or the generator config.

call to confirm the file exists, understand its current content, and locate the exact lines to change before any write or replace call. This step prevents writing stale content and gives the model the context it needs to construct a correct replacement.
Prefer str_replace_editor over write_file for edits to existing files.
A surgical replacement is faster (fewer tokens, less hallucination surface area) and less risky (unchanged lines are untouched). Reserve
for creating new files or for cases where the file structure needs substantial rearrangement.
Make old_str wide enough to be unique.
Include one or two lines of surrounding context when the target string could appear in multiple places. A good heuristic: include the function signature or the preceding conditional in
Verify each edit with a read-back.
call, issue a windowed read to confirm that the file now contains exactly what was intended. This catches whitespace mismatches, off-by-one insertions, and encoding issues before they compound.
Before renaming a symbol or changing a function signature across the codebase, run a
call to enumerate every file that will be affected. This prevents missed call sites and helps the agent plan the edit sequence.

An agent that edits 30 files in one turn without verification checkpoints is hard to debug. Prefer smaller batches — edit 3-5 files, verify, continue — so that any error can be caught and recovered from without unwinding a large cascade of changes.
The primary performance bottleneck in file-editing-tool workflows is not disk I/O (which is milliseconds) but context window consumption. Every

result adds tokens to the conversation, and long conversations hit latency and cost penalties. The table below shows approximate token costs for common operations assuming an average Python codebase with 50 characters per line:

The practical implication: agents should read files in windows whenever possible. A heuristic used by several production coding agents is to start with a 50-line window around the suspected edit location and expand only if more context is needed. This keeps most reads under 500 tokens.

step dominates latency — ripgrep is fast (often sub-second for a 100 MB codebase) but the result must be serialized, injected into the context, and parsed by the model. Capping results at 25-50 matches and using narrow glob patterns (e.g.,

In automated pipelines running many agents in parallel (e.g., a CI bot that opens one agent per pull request), filesystem contention can become a bottleneck if agents share a working directory. The solution is to give each agent an isolated directory via Docker, a git worktree, or a copy-on-write filesystem snapshot. Each agent operates against its own copy and the results are merged afterward.
Production coding agent; Edit enforces uniqueness
LLM-agnostic; uses unified diff or SEARCH/REPLACE blocks
Open-source; supports Docker sandbox and browser tools
File tools via VS Code extension API
IDE-integrated; shows diffs inline in editor
Research agent; open = read + cursor navigation
Generic; no str_replace; intended for simple tasks
Several strategies exist for giving AI agents the ability to change files. They differ in precision, token cost, error recoverability, and implementation complexity.
Medium — apply errors can be cryptic
The practical recommendation is to use
as the default for incremental edits and

only for new file creation. AST-based tools become worthwhile for large-scale automated refactors (renaming a class that appears in 200 files) but add a significant tooling dependency. Shell-based approaches should be avoided in agent contexts because their error modes are opaque and they have the widest blast radius.
The most critical security property of a file editing tool executor is path containment. An agent instructed, or manipulated via prompt injection, to read

must be stopped at the executor level, not relied on the model's judgment. The correct defense is to resolve all paths to absolute paths and reject any that fall outside the designated workspace root.

import pathlib

WORKSPACE = pathlib.Path("/workspace/my-project").resolve()

def safe_resolve(user_path: str) -> pathlib.Path:
    # Resolve symlinks and normalize; raises ValueError if outside WORKSPACE
    candidate = (WORKSPACE / user_path).resolve()
    candidate.relative_to(WORKSPACE)  # raises ValueError if outside
    return candidate
Even with path containment, a symlink inside the workspace that points to a file outside it can bypass the check if the executor follows symlinks before validation. The
call follows symlinks; to prevent symlink escapes, check both the resolved path and, for writes, that no component of the path is a symlink pointing outside the workspace.
Write operations should be atomic to prevent partial writes from leaving files in a corrupt state. The standard pattern is to write to a temporary file in the same directory and then call

(POSIX atomic rename). On POSIX systems, this guarantees that any reader of the file sees either the old content or the new content, never a partial write. The rename must be on the same filesystem as the target file to be atomic.
Always specify the encoding explicitly when reading and writing files. The default encoding varies by OS and locale, which can cause silent data corruption. Use
when reading files that may contain non-UTF-8 bytes (binary segments, Latin-1 comments in legacy code). Never use
— it silently drops bytes and can mask the presence of binary content that would corrupt the file on write.

In automated CI contexts, running the file editing tool executor inside a Docker container or a gVisor sandbox limits the blast radius of a misbehaving agent to the contents of the container. Combine containerization with: read-only mounts for files the agent should not modify (dependency caches, mounted secrets), network isolation to prevent data exfiltration, and resource limits (CPU, memory, disk write quota) to prevent runaway agents from exhausting system resources.
together enable the full range of code-editing tasks from targeted bug fixes to multi-file refactors.
Surgical string replacement is token-efficient, auditable, and safe. Use it by default for edits to existing files; reserve
The old string must appear exactly once. Include surrounding context lines as anchors to satisfy this requirement when the target text is short or common.
call before any edit confirms current state, prevents overwriting stale content, and gives the model the context needed to construct a correct replacement.

Every executor must resolve paths and reject anything outside the workspace root. This must be enforced in code, not relied on model judgment — prompt injection attacks will attempt to escape the sandbox.
Use windowed reads for large files:
Offset and limit parameters keep per-read token costs manageable. Reading a 50-line window costs ~375 tokens versus ~2,500 tokens for a full 200-line file read.
Write to a temp file, then rename — this ensures readers always see complete content, never a partial write.
After every write or replace, issue a windowed read to confirm the file contains exactly the intended content before proceeding to the next step.
